mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the nature, scope, and diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1–A.2) and surveys all its major divisions — metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, philosophy of mind, political philosophy, aesthetics, philosophy of language, science, religion, and mathematics — directly mapping to paths B.1 through B.16. Provides the broadest overview of what the section is about.

Directly covers the history, nature, scope, and problems of epistemology (B.3.a, B.3.c), its relation to logic, philosophy of science, and philosophy of mind (B.3.b), and major schools including skepticism, empiricism, rationalism, foundationalism, and coherentism.

Aristotle's written corpus encompasses treatises constituting major forms of philosophical exposition (A.3) and foundational contributions to metaphysics and substance theory (B.1), philosophy of biology (B.2.e), philosophical anthropology/psychology (B.5), and virtue ethics (B.6). His Organon is also foundational to B.10.

Kant's Critiques directly address epistemology and the limits of knowledge (B.3), deontological moral philosophy (B.6), political philosophy including perpetual peace (B.7), and aesthetics and teleological judgment (B.8), making him central to four major divisions of philosophy.

Time is a core problem in metaphysics (B.1.c — the nature of temporal existence, identity over time) and in the philosophy of nature (B.2.c — basic aspects of the natural order; B.2.d — philosophy of physics, including special and general relativistic time dilation). The article explicitly spans philosophical and scientific conceptions.

Reason as a cognitive capacity is central to diverse views of philosophical method (A.2) and is a key problem in epistemology (B.3.c — sources and validity of knowledge) and philosophy of mind (B.4.c — the nature of rational cognition and its relation to emotion and intuition).

Covers the major theories of thinking — Platonism, Aristotelianism, computationalism, behaviorism, inner-speech hypotheses — squarely within the philosophy of mind (B.4.a, B.4.c), and spans phenomenology, cognitive psychology, and AI, all of which B.4.b cross-references.

Directly treats the history, nature, and scope of philosophy of mind (B.4.a), its relation to neuroscience, cognitive science, and empirical disciplines (B.4.b), and core problems including the mind–body problem, consciousness, and mental disorder (B.4.c).

Descartes' method of radical doubt and foundationalism (B.3), mind-body dualism (B.4), and metaphysical arguments for God's existence (B.1) are central to the divisions of philosophy; his dialogical treatises exemplify a major form of philosophical exposition (A.3).

Mathematical proof is the foundational object of the philosophy of mathematics (B.11) and connects directly to the philosophy of logic (B.10) through proof theory and the relation between formal systems and mathematical truth.

Provides a comprehensive treatment of moral philosophy covering the history and types of ethics including metaethics and normative ethics (B.6.a), its relation to other disciplines (B.6.b), and key problems and theories — consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics (B.6.c).

Socrates developed the elenctic dialogue as a distinctive form of philosophical exposition (A.3) and his Socratic method is central to epistemology (B.3) and moral philosophy (B.6); his legacy shaped the entire subsequent tradition addressed in B.

Plato's dialogues are the paradigm of philosophical exposition in dialogue form (A.3); his Theory of Forms anchors metaphysics (B.1) and epistemology (B.3), and the Republic is a foundational text of political philosophy (B.7).

Aquinas' Summa Theologiae treats metaphysics and natural theology (B.1.b — relation to logic and natural theology), moral philosophy and natural law (B.6), and the philosophy of religion (B.14) through systematic treatise form.

The Enlightenment crystallized distinctive philosophical conceptions (A.1) and methods centered on reason and empirical inquiry (A.2), and generated major advances in moral philosophy (B.6) and political philosophy including natural rights, constitutionalism, and social contract theory (B.7).

Good and evil addresses core metaphysical problems including theodicy and the ontological status of evil (B.1.c), central normative and metaethical problems (B.6.c — moral realism, naturalism, cognitivism), and the philosophy of religion (B.14 — the problem of evil in theism).

Covers the philosophical analysis of knowledge including the Gettier problem, foundationalism, coherentism, and the sources of justified belief (B.3.a, B.3.c), as well as criteria of truth applicable to philosophical thought (A.4).

Machiavelli is a central figure in the history of political philosophy (B.7.c), separating empirical analysis of power from normative virtue frameworks and thereby transforming the discipline; his work bears directly on the relation of political philosophy to political science (B.7.a).

Freud's structural model of the mind (id, ego, superego) and the unconscious directly engage problems in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c), while his psychosexual theory of human nature connects to philosophical anthropology (B.5.c).

Gödel's incompleteness theorems set permanent limits on what can be proved within any formal system, directly shaping the philosophy of logic (B.10) and the philosophy of mathematics (B.11) by demonstrating the irreducibility of mathematical truth to formal provability.

Surveys the development of philosophical inquiry across all major traditions (A), illustrating diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1) and the major forms of exposition from Presocratic fragments through Platonic dialogues to systematic treatises (A.3).

Directly covers the history, nature, and scope of aesthetics (B.8.a), its development and key approaches (B.8.b), problems including the ontology of artworks and aesthetic judgment (B.8.c), and its relation to other disciplines including ethics and psychology (B.8.d); also maps to B.12 (philosophy of art).

Directly addresses the contested definition, classification, and ontology of art and the distinction between fine, applied, and decorative arts, mapping to the philosophy of art (B.12) and its overlap with aesthetics (B.8).

Directly covers the history, nature, and scope of metaphysics (B.1.a), its relation to ethics, logic, and natural theology (B.1.b), and core problems including universals, identity, causation, space, time, and modality (B.1.c).

Covers the major non-Western philosophical traditions that embody diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1) and alternative frameworks in metaphysics (B.1) and ethics (B.6), providing comparative context for the Western philosophical divisions outlined in B.

Political science is explicitly named in the outline as the discipline to which political philosophy must be related (B.7.a); the article defines how empirical political science diverges from normative political philosophy, directly illuminating that boundary.

Mathematics as a discipline is the subject matter of the philosophy of mathematics (B.11), covering the axiomatic method, pure versus applied mathematics, foundational questions, and historical development that grounds philosophical inquiry into mathematical truth and method.

Logic is the direct subject of the philosophy of logic (B.10), covering formal and informal reasoning, metalogic, and the full historical sweep from Aristotelian syllogistics to contemporary proof theory.

Psychology is the primary empirical discipline to which philosophy of mind (B.4.b) and philosophical anthropology (B.5.b) must be related, making it essential context for understanding those divisions even though the article itself is not philosophical.

Truth theories — correspondence, coherence, pragmatism, deflation — are directly relevant to criteria of meaning and truth in philosophical thought (A.4) and to problems in epistemology including what justification and knowledge consist in (B.3.c).

Ontology is the core of metaphysics (B.1.a), addressing what entities exist and how they are classified — including universals, abstract objects, and possible worlds — which are among the key problems in metaphysics (B.1.c).

Education is the subject matter of the philosophy of education (B.16), a priority coverage path; the article covers the aims and ideologies of education which are the objects of philosophical analysis in B.16.

Avicenna's Book of Healing covers Peripatetic metaphysics (B.1) and psychology of the soul (B.4); his natural theology and proof of the Necessary Existent engages the philosophy of religion (B.14), bridging Islamic and European scholastic philosophy.

The scientific method is the primary subject of the philosophy of science (B.13), covering falsifiability, empiricism, rationalism, and methodological debates; it also bears on the relation of the philosophy of nature to science and metaphysics (B.2.b).

The concept of nature is the subject of the philosophy of nature (B.2.a), including its scope and the philosophical tensions between humans as part of or apart from nature (B.2.c — basic aspects of the natural order).

Human rights are a central topic of political philosophy (B.7) through natural rights theory and social contract, and of moral philosophy in debates over moral realism, universalism, and cultural relativism (B.6.c).

Cicero's philosophical treatises adapting Hellenistic schools into Latin represent a major tradition of philosophical exposition (A.3); his works on moral duty, justice, and natural law connect to political philosophy (B.7) and ethics (B.6).

Locke's Essay is foundational to epistemology (B.3 — tabula rasa, sources of ideas); his Two Treatises ground political philosophy in natural rights and consent (B.7); his theory of personal identity engages philosophy of mind (B.4); and his advocacy of church-state separation is relevant to philosophy of law (B.15).

The arts as a category directly supplies the content of the philosophy of art (B.12) and connects to aesthetics (B.8) through aesthetic theory and art criticism, covering the practice and production context for philosophical inquiry.

Emotion is a central problem in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c — the relation of feelings to thought, motivation, and rational agency) and bears on moral psychology and metaethics (B.6.c — moral motivation and the role of feeling in ethical judgment).

Galileo's work relates the philosophy of nature to experimental science and to Copernican mechanics (B.2.b, B.2.d — philosophy of physics), and his confrontation with the Church is a foundational episode in the philosophy of science (B.13).

Darwin's theory of evolution is central to the philosophy of biology (B.2.e), raises problems about human nature in philosophical anthropology (B.5.c), and is a key episode in the philosophy of science (B.13) regarding the demarcation and history of scientific theorizing.

Property has a rich philosophical tradition from Locke's labor theory through Hume, Marx, and Proudhon, engaging political philosophy (B.7), distributive justice in ethics (B.6.c), and the philosophy of law (B.15).

Social science is the body of empirical disciplines to which philosophical anthropology (B.5.b) must be related; understanding its scope and methods contextualizes what philosophical anthropology contributes beyond empirical inquiry.

Physical and cultural anthropology are explicitly named in B.5.b as the disciplines to which philosophical anthropology must relate; the article's coverage of human evolution, culture, and kinship provides the empirical backdrop for philosophical anthropology's problems.
